name: Looking Glass Project
description: The Looking Glass Project is a conspiracy theory alleging the existence of a highly classified US military program involving advanced technology capable of observing potential futures. Proponents claim this technology, often described as utilizing exotic physics and possibly extraterrestrial artifacts, allowed the US government to foresee and potentially manipulate events. The theory gained traction in the late 1990s and early 2000s, fueled by internet forums and alternative media. Key figures associated with the theory often cite anecdotal evidence, leaked documents (often of questionable authenticity), and interpretations of ancient prophecies as proof. They suggest the project was shut down or reconfigured due to unforeseen consequences or ethical concerns related to altering timelines. Mainstream scientific and historical perspectives dismiss the Looking Glass Project as pseudoscience and a product of speculative fiction. No credible evidence supports the existence of such a program, and the claims made by proponents contradict established principles of physics and historical records. The lack of verifiable evidence, reliance on unsubstantiated claims, and the inherent difficulty of proving or disproving such a secretive project contribute to its enduring appeal within conspiracy circles.
subtitle: Alleged US Military Time-Viewing Technology

claims.claim: The US military developed technology to view future timelines.
claims.evidence: Alleged leaked documents, anecdotal testimonies from supposed insiders.
claims.criticism: No credible evidence exists; documents are often forgeries or misinterpretations. Testimonies lack verification and are often contradictory.

claims.claim: The technology used exotic physics and possibly extraterrestrial artifacts.
claims.evidence: Claims of reverse-engineered technology, connections to ancient prophecies and esoteric knowledge.
claims.criticism: No scientific basis for such claims; relies on unsubstantiated assertions about extraterrestrial involvement and misinterpretations of ancient texts.

claims.claim: The project was shut down due to unforeseen consequences or ethical concerns.
claims.evidence: Lack of continued visible activity, claims of catastrophic future events seen through the Looking Glass.
claims.criticism: Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. The lack of public information is consistent with standard military secrecy, regardless of whether the project existed.

keyFigures.name: Bill Wood
keyFigures.role: One of the early proponents who discussed the project online.

keyFigures.name: Dan Burisch
keyFigures.role: Claimed involvement in classified projects, including those related to time travel and extraterrestrial life.

keyFigures.name: David Wilcock
keyFigures.role: Prominent figure in the alternative media community who has discussed the Looking Glass Project in his presentations and writings.

keyFigures.name: Michael Shermer
keyFigures.role: Science writer and skeptic who critiques paranormal and conspiracy claims.

timeline.event: Initial emergence of the Looking Glass Project theory online.
Increased popularity and spread of the theory through alternative media.
Continued discussion and speculation within conspiracy theory communities.
